title: Cache Optimization
rules: name the hot traversal before changing layout
optimize access order and ownership before instruction-level tweaks
treat false sharing as a write-ownership problem
preserve semantic and phase boundaries while improving locality
measure cache-sensitive changes against the real workload
procedure: Identify the hot loop or worker range.
Map read and write order to physical layout.
Check hot/cold mixing, pointer chasing, and cross-thread write adjacency.
Choose traversal reorder, staging, partitioning, or layout change.
Re-measure locality-sensitive pass cost and scaling behavior.

primary_when: request is specifically about cache locality, false sharing, or cache miss reduction
request is about hot-path traversal order and layout
must_not_own_when: request is mainly about plugin lifecycle, IMGUI, or metadata parsing

solves: aligning data layout and traversal order with cache behavior
reducing wasted movement across memory on hot paths
mitigating cross-thread write contention such as false sharing
does_not_solve: cold-path cleanup with no measurable locality issue
instruction-level micro-optimization detached from access patterns
parallel scaling problems caused by scheduler design alone

core_principles: Optimize access order before micro-tuning instructions.
Keep related hot data close in memory when they are consumed together.
Reduce shared write contention and accidental cross-thread cache-line traffic.
Treat locality as a property of traversal plus layout, not of layout alone.
Re-measure after changes because cache wins are workload-dependent.
constraints: Do not invent low-level tweaks without identifying the actual hot traversal path.
Do not trade away maintainable ownership boundaries for tiny unmeasured gains.
Avoid cache tuning on cold code or on code dominated by unrelated costs.
Keep cross-thread writes explicit so false sharing risks are inspectable.
decision_rules: If data is consumed together in the same pass, keep it adjacent or stage it into adjacent buffers.
If multiple threads update neighboring state, inspect false sharing before adding more synchronization.
If the same loop both reads hot data and jumps into cold side structures, split the path or pre-stage the data.
If locality improvements require whole-pipeline staging, validate the extra transform cost.
If the bottleneck is dominated by synchronization or allocation, fix that first instead of forcing cache folklore.

preferred_patterns: stable hot-path traversal over contiguous or locality-aware buffers
thread partitioning that minimizes overlapping writes
staging buffers that reshape sparse source data into dense execution order
loop ordering based on the dominant access pattern
avoid_patterns: interleaving unrelated hot and cold writes in the same tight loop
shared arrays with neighboring write ownership spread across workers
micro-optimizing code shape while leaving access order unchanged
assuming cache behavior can be fixed without understanding traversal

recipes.name: Traversal Reordering
recipes.when: The same data set is revisited in an order that causes unnecessary memory jumps.
steps: Map the current read and write order to the data layout.
Reorder the loop or reshape the working set to improve adjacency.
Re-measure the pass and verify no higher-level cost regressed.

recipes.name: False Sharing Reduction
recipes.when: Multiple workers update nearby memory and scaling collapses under contention.
steps: Identify the write ownership of each worker.
Separate frequently written state by partition, stripe, or staging buffer.
Merge results through an explicit reduction step if needed.
Measure scaling again under representative worker counts.

pitfalls: Talking about cache friendliness without naming the actual traversal pattern.
Forcing low-level layout tricks into cold or low-impact code.
Ignoring false sharing while focusing only on read locality.
Breaking ownership clarity for locality gains that were never measured.
review_checklist: Is the hot traversal path explicitly identified?
Does the layout reflect how the data is actually consumed?
Are cross-thread writes partitioned to avoid accidental cache-line contention?
Were locality changes measured against the real workload?
Did the optimization preserve explicit ownership and inspectable control flow?

references.title: Cache Optimization
references.interpretation: Use for locality heuristics, false-sharing analysis, and cache-focused review.

references.title: Data-Oriented Design
references.interpretation: Use when cache behavior depends on broader layout and staging decisions.
